name: Anise & Walnut Biscotti
recipeIngredient: 1/2 cup Unsalted butter, melted and cooled
8 ounces Bittersweet or semisweet chocolate, optional
recipeInstructions:
1. Blend bread and cake flours, walnuts, baking powder, baking soda, and anise seeds in a bowl.
2. Melt butter and set aside to cool.
3. Beat 2 eggs and 1 additional egg yolk with sugar until smooth. Reserve the remaining egg white separately.
4. Stir in lemon zest, orange zest, and anise extract. Add melted butter and mix until smooth.
5. Gradually add dry ingredients and mix until a smooth dough forms.
6. Form the dough into two 14-inch logs and place on a parchment-lined baking sheet.
7. Press down lightly on the tops of the logs to flatten slightly.
8. Brush the logs with the reserved egg white.
9. Bake at 375 degrees F. for about 30 minutes, or until light brown and still yield to gentle pressure.
10. Remove from oven and reduce temperature to 325 degrees F.
11. While the logs are still warm, cut diagonally into 1/2-inch slices.
12. Return the slices to the baking sheet, arranging on their sides.
13. Return to the oven and bake until lightly browned, about 15 minutes more.
14. Remove from oven and cool on wire racks.
15. For chocolate coating:
- Place 2/3 cup of chocolate in a stainless steel bowl and spread evenly over the bottom and sides of the bowl.
- Cover the bowl with plastic wrap.
- Fill a medium saucepan one-third full with water, bring to a simmer over high heat, and remove from heat.
- Set the bowl over the simmering water. Let stand until 80% of the chocolate has melted.
16. Do not let the temperature of the chocolate drop below 88 degrees on a candy thermometer.
17. If it does, place the bowl back over warm water and continue stirring until smooth and glossy.
18. Have a clean sheet of waxed paper ready.
19. Dip half of each cookie in the chocolate, removing excess by scraping against the side of the bowl.
20. Set cookies on waxed paper to let the chocolate set, about 3 hours, or place them in the freezer for about 20 minutes before serving.
